expanded_description: Apply sine, cosine, and tangent ratios systematically to solve for unknown side lengths in right triangles, mastering the selection of the appropriate trigonometric function based on given information and developing fluency in algebraic manipulation of trigonometric equations.

core_concept.principle: Strategic Ratio Selection for Side Finding
core_concept.explanation: Finding missing sides requires matching the given angle and known side to the correct trigonometric ratio. The key is identifying which two sides are involved (one known, one unknown) relative to the given angle, then selecting SOH, CAH, or TOA accordingly. This systematic approach eliminates guesswork and ensures accurate solutions.

image.alt: Flowchart showing decision process: identify known side position → identify unknown side position → choose ratio containing both → solve
image.caption: Systematic ratio selection based on side positions relative to the given angle

image.alt: Three right triangles side-by-side showing: (1) angle and hypotenuse given, find opposite; (2) angle and adjacent given, find hypotenuse; (3) angle and opposite given, find adjacent
image.caption: Every side-finding problem fits one of these three patterns

sections.introduction: All trigonometric side-finding problems fall into three categories based on which side is given: hypotenuse, adjacent, or opposite. Mastering these three scenarios provides a complete toolkit for solving any right triangle side-length problem.

key_concept.definition: Scenario 1: Given angle and hypotenuse → use sine or cosine. Scenario 2: Given angle and adjacent → use cosine or tangent. Scenario 3: Given angle and opposite → use sine or tangent. The choice between the two options depends on which side you're solving for.
key_concept.context: This framework eliminates confusion by providing a clear decision tree. Once you identify which side is given and which side you need, the appropriate ratio is determined.

image.alt: 3x3 matrix showing given side types in rows, target side types in columns, with appropriate trig ratio in each cell
image.caption: This matrix provides instant ratio selection for any combination

principles.statement: When the hypotenuse is known, use sine to find the opposite side ($\text{opp} = \text{hyp} \cdot \sin \theta$) or cosine to find the adjacent side ($\text{adj} = \text{hyp} \cdot \cos \theta$).
principles.application: These are the most straightforward calculations since they involve direct multiplication without division.

image.alt: Triangle with hypotenuse labeled, arrows showing multiplication by sin θ to get opposite and cos θ to get adjacent
image.caption: Hypotenuse is the starting point for finding either leg

principles.statement: When the adjacent side is known, use cosine to find the hypotenuse ($\text{hyp} = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\cos \theta}$) or tangent to find the opposite side ($\text{opp} = \text{adj} \cdot \tan \theta$).
principles.application: Finding the hypotenuse requires division, while finding the opposite requires multiplication. Be careful with the division operation.

image.alt: Triangle with adjacent side labeled, showing two calculation paths
image.caption: Adjacent side connects to both hypotenuse (via cosine) and opposite (via tangent)

principles.statement: When the opposite side is known, use sine to find the hypotenuse ($\text{hyp} = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\sin \theta}$) or tangent to find the adjacent side ($\text{adj} = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\tan \theta}$).
principles.application: Both calculations require division. This is the most error-prone scenario, so double-check your algebra.

image.alt: Triangle with opposite side labeled, showing division operations to find other sides
image.caption: Opposite side requires division to find either adjacent or hypotenuse

worked_examples.title: Scenario 1: Hypotenuse Given, Find Opposite
worked_examples.problem: In right triangle $ABC$ with right angle at $C$, $m\angle A = 28^\circ$ and hypotenuse $AB = 15$ cm. Find the length of side $BC$ (opposite to $\angle A$).

step_by_step.calculation: Given: angle $A = 28^\circ$, hypotenuse = 15. Find: opposite side $BC$.

step_by_step.explanation: Select ratio containing opposite and hypotenuse

image.alt: Triangle ABC with angle 28°, hypotenuse 15, opposite side BC labeled with calculation
image.caption: Multiplying hypotenuse by sine gives the opposite side

worked_examples.title: Scenario 2: Adjacent Given, Find Hypotenuse
worked_examples.problem: A ramp makes a $15^\circ$ angle with the ground. The horizontal distance (adjacent side) is 20 feet. Find the length of the ramp (hypotenuse).

step_by_step.calculation: Given: angle = $15^\circ$, adjacent = 20 ft. Find: hypotenuse (ramp length).

step_by_step.explanation: Select ratio containing adjacent and hypotenuse

worked_examples.final_answer: The ramp is approximately 20.7 feet long
worked_examples.concept_applied: Cosine ratio with adjacent given, solving for hypotenuse

image.alt: Ramp diagram with 15° angle, horizontal 20 ft, ramp length x labeled
image.caption: Dividing adjacent by cosine yields the hypotenuse

worked_examples.title: Scenario 3: Opposite Given, Find Adjacent
worked_examples.problem: From a point on the ground, the angle of elevation to the top of a building is $52^\circ$. The building is 80 feet tall. How far from the building is the observation point?

step_by_step.calculation: Given: angle = $52^\circ$, opposite (height) = 80 ft. Find: adjacent (distance).

step_by_step.explanation: Select ratio containing opposite and adjacent

worked_examples.final_answer: The observation point is approximately 62.5 feet from the building
worked_examples.concept_applied: Tangent ratio with opposite given, solving for adjacent

image.alt: Building diagram with 52° angle of elevation, height 80 ft, distance x labeled
image.caption: Dividing opposite by tangent yields the adjacent distance

image.alt: Three equation examples showing isolation steps: multiplication, division, and rearrangement
image.caption: Proper algebraic manipulation is essential before calculator use

sections.introduction: Before reaching for the calculator, you must algebraically isolate the unknown side. This prevents calculation errors and ensures you're computing the correct expression.

key_concept.definition: Standard forms: To find opposite: $\text{opp} = \text{hyp} \cdot \sin \theta$ or $\text{opp} = \text{adj} \cdot \tan \theta$. To find adjacent: $\text{adj} = \text{hyp} \cdot \cos \theta$ or $\text{adj} = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\tan \theta}$. To find hypotenuse: $\text{hyp} = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\sin \theta}$ or $\text{hyp} = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\cos \theta}$.
key_concept.context: Memorizing these six forms allows you to skip the algebraic step and go directly to calculation, but understanding the derivation prevents errors.

image.alt: Reference card showing all six isolated forms organized by target side
image.caption: These six formulas cover every possible side-finding scenario

principles.statement: When the unknown is in the numerator (opposite or adjacent being found), you multiply. When the unknown is in the denominator (hypotenuse being found from opposite or adjacent), you divide.
principles.application: This pattern helps you remember whether to multiply or divide without memorizing all six forms.
principles.example: Finding opposite: multiply (opp = hyp × sin θ). Finding hypotenuse: divide (hyp = opp ÷ sin θ).

image.alt: Equation examples showing numerator vs denominator positions
image.caption: Position of the unknown determines the operation

worked_examples.title: Multi-Step Problem: Finding Both Legs
worked_examples.problem: In right triangle $XYZ$ with right angle at $Z$, $m\angle X = 43^\circ$ and hypotenuse $XY = 25$ m. Find both legs $XZ$ and $YZ$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using both sine and cosine with hypotenuse given

image.alt: Triangle XYZ with hypotenuse 25, both legs calculated and labeled
image.caption: When hypotenuse is given, both legs are found by multiplication

image.alt: Five-step flowchart: Draw/label → Identify given/unknown → Select ratio → Isolate variable → Calculate
image.caption: Following this sequence ensures accurate solutions every time

sections.introduction: A disciplined approach to trigonometric side-finding prevents errors and builds confidence. This five-step framework works for every problem type.

process_flow.description: Sketch the triangle if not provided. Mark the right angle, label the given angle with its measure, and mark all known side lengths. Label the unknown with a variable (usually $x$).

image.alt: Blank triangle being filled in with angle measures, side lengths, and variable
image.caption: A complete diagram prevents misidentification of sides

process_flow.process: Identify given angle and side positions
process_flow.description: Circle the given angle. Label all three sides as opposite, adjacent, or hypotenuse relative to this angle. Note which side is given and which you need to find.

image.alt: Triangle with reference angle circled and sides labeled with their positions
image.caption: Correct labeling is the foundation of ratio selection

process_flow.description: Use the decision matrix: which two sides are involved (one known, one unknown)? Choose SOH, CAH, or TOA accordingly. Write the ratio formula.
process_flow.mechanism: Match known and unknown to ratio components

image.alt: Decision matrix showing side combinations and corresponding ratios
image.caption: The matrix eliminates guesswork in function selection

process_flow.description: Plug in the known values (angle measure and side length). Algebraically isolate the unknown variable on one side of the equation before calculating.

image.alt: Equation showing substitution and isolation steps
image.caption: Isolation before calculation prevents order-of-operations errors

process_flow.description: Use calculator in degree mode to compute the trigonometric value. Multiply or divide as required. Round appropriately. Optionally verify with Pythagorean Theorem if two sides are now known.

image.alt: Calculator display showing computation and final answer
image.caption: Verification catches calculation errors before submission

worked_examples.title: Real-World Application: Ladder Safety
worked_examples.problem: A 24-foot ladder leans against a wall at a $75^\circ$ angle with the ground. How high up the wall does the ladder reach? How far is the base from the wall?

step_by_step.calculation: Right triangle: hypotenuse (ladder) = 24 ft, angle with ground = $75^\circ$, unknown: opposite (height) and adjacent (base distance).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Using both sine and cosine with hypotenuse given in real-world context

image.alt: Ladder diagram with 75° angle, 24 ft ladder, height and base distance labeled
image.caption: Real-world problems often require finding multiple unknowns

categories.description: Systematic mistakes that lead to incorrect side lengths.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Using the wrong ratio (mixing up which sides SOH CAH TOA refer to), forgetting to isolate the variable before calculating, calculator in radian mode, dividing when should multiply (or vice versa), and rounding intermediate values too early.

image.alt: Three error examples with red corrections showing proper technique
image.caption: Recognizing these patterns prevents systematic calculation failures

examples.explanation: Error: Student calculates $\sin 40^\circ \approx 0.6428$ then multiplies by 15 to get 9.64, but the equation was $\sin 40^\circ = \dfrac{x}{15}$, so $x = 15 \sin 40^\circ$ (correct answer). Always isolate first!

image.alt: Equation showing incorrect direct calculation vs correct isolation then calculation
image.caption: Algebraic isolation must precede numerical calculation

examples.explanation: Error: Given hypotenuse = 20 and angle = 30°, student calculates opposite = $\dfrac{20}{\sin 30^\circ} = 40$ (wrong). Correct: opposite = $20 \cdot \sin 30^\circ = 10$. When finding a leg from hypotenuse, multiply, don't divide.
examples.note: Remember: hypotenuse is always the longest side. If your calculated leg is longer than the hypotenuse, you've made an error.

summary: Finding unknown side lengths using trigonometry requires a systematic five-step approach: (1) draw and label the triangle completely, (2) identify the reference angle and label all sides as opposite, adjacent, or hypotenuse, (3) select the appropriate trigonometric ratio (SOH, CAH, or TOA) based on which sides are known and unknown, (4) substitute known values and algebraically isolate the unknown variable, (5) calculate using a calculator in degree mode and verify if possible. The three fundamental scenarios are: hypotenuse given (multiply by sin or cos), adjacent given (divide by cos or multiply by tan), and opposite given (divide by sin or tan). Critical success factors include correct side labeling relative to the reference angle, proper algebraic isolation before calculation, and ensuring the calculator is in degree mode. Common errors include using the wrong ratio, dividing when multiplying is required (or vice versa), and rounding intermediate values. Mastering these techniques enables solution of real-world problems involving heights, distances, inclines, and indirect measurements. The six standard isolated forms ($\text{opp} = \text{hyp} \cdot \sin \theta$, $\text{adj} = \text{hyp} \cdot \cos \theta$, $\text{opp} = \text{adj} \cdot \tan \theta$, $\text{hyp} = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\sin \theta}$, $\text{hyp} = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\cos \theta}$, $\text{adj} = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\tan \theta}$) provide a complete toolkit for any right triangle side-finding problem.
